nativeRange.habitat: Rocky shoreline (mbuna habitat) along the northwestern and western coasts of Lake Malawi; found among sediment-free boulder piles and rock faces where aufwuchs-covered surfaces provide foraging

decorPreferences: dense rockwork with caves and crevices to break line of sight
stacked boulders and slate creating multiple territories
minimal or no planting (plants typically destroyed or uprooted)
no open bare-bottomed areas — territorial boundaries require physical structure

diet.notes: Naturally grazes aufwuchs — the algae and associated micro-invertebrates coating lake rocks. In the aquarium, the core diet should be spirulina-based flakes or pellets, algae wafers, and blanched greens (spinach, zucchini, peas). High-protein foods (bloodworms, high-meat pellets) should be offered sparingly and infrequently; excessive animal protein has been linked to 'Malawi bloat' (internal protozoan bloom) in mbuna. A diet of ≥70% plant/algae matter is strongly advised.

goodWith: other mbuna of similar size and aggression level (Pseudotropheus, Labidochromis, Cynotilapia)
other Lake Malawi cichlids of comparable temperament
robust catfish suitable for hard alkaline water (Synodontis multipunctatus)
avoidWith: Metriaclima estherae (Red Zebra) — hybridizes readily; trade stock is frequently contaminated with hybrids
conspecific males (intense territory fights without sufficient space and cover)
peaceful community fish (tetras, livebearers, rasboras — will be harassed and killed)
haplochromine cichlids significantly smaller than the cobalt blue
Aulonocara (peacock) cichlids in small tanks — chronic harassment

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase specimen image of Maylandia callainos; shows characteristic uniform cobalt blue coloration of male.
Wikimedia Commons image; verify availability and attribution before use.

summary: A Lake Malawi mbuna notable for uniform cobalt-blue coloration in both sexes — rare in a group where most species show sexual dichromatism. Reaches ~12 cm, moderately to strongly aggressive, and best kept in a species-only or mbuna-community tank with heavy rockwork. Frequent hybridization with M. estherae in the trade means sourcing from reputable breeders matters.
careNotes:
The Cobalt Blue Cichlid is endemic to the rocky littoral zone of Lake Malawi, where it forages on aufwuchs — the algal mat and associated microfauna coating boulder surfaces. Water chemistry must reflect the lake's famously hard, alkaline, and stable conditions: pH 7.8–8.6, moderate to high hardness (gH 10–20, kH 8–16), temperature 24–28°C. Large water changes using untreated tap water low in carbonates should be avoided; buffering via crushed coral, limestone rock, or rift lake salt mixes is often recommended.

A minimum tank of 200 L (120 cm footprint) is the usual starting point for a small mbuna community, though larger is strongly preferred. The tank must be heavily aquascaped with rocks and slate stacked to create numerous caves and visual breaks; without this, dominant males will kill subordinates. Keeping a higher ratio of females to males (1 male : 3–4 females) and overstocking relative to a typical cichlid tank helps distribute aggression. Removing and re-introducing all fish when adding new individuals resets territorial claims.

The genus designation of this species remains contested in the scientific literature: Stauffer et al. (1997) placed it in Metriaclima, later revised to Maylandia by Stauffer (2004); Konings and co-authors have continued using Metriaclima as the preferred name in more recent works (including Konings & Stauffer 2018). Both names are encountered in the hobby and in trade, and both refer to the same species. FishBase currently uses Maylandia callainos as the accepted name.

A significant practical concern is hybridization: M. callainos crosses freely with M. estherae (Red Zebra Cichlid) and potentially other Maylandia species. Hybrid males often display mixed or aberrant coloration. Purchasing from specialist African cichlid breeders rather than big-box pet stores is advisable to obtain pure stock.

Do not keep with slow, peaceful, or small fish. Mbuna aggression is not incidental — it is a deeply ingrained behavioral trait that cannot be selected out through tank conditions alone.
breedingNotes:
Maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning follows typical mbuna 'T-position' courtship: the male displays intensely to the female over a flat rock or sand patch, fertilization occurs as the female collects the eggs, and she then holds the entire brood in her buccal cavity for approximately 21–28 days until the fry are large enough to be released. Clutch size is typically 15–40 eggs. The holding female should ideally be moved to a separate tank to reduce harassment; forced spitting of the brood is possible but stressful and risks fry mortality.

Fry are released at a relatively advanced free-swimming stage and can accept finely crushed spirulina flake or micro-pellets immediately. The mother will often re-accept fry into her mouth for several days post-release if threatened. Breedng difficulty is moderate: spawning is straightforward in a well-maintained mbuna setup, but separating the holding female and raising fry successfully requires some experience. Hybridization risk with co-housed M. estherae or other Maylandia species is high — keep only one Maylandia species per tank if pure breeding stock is the goal.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Maylandia callainos
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided habitat, water parameters, diet, tank requirements, compatibility, and breeding notes for M. callainos. Native range, taxonomy, and mbuna behavioral context cross-checked against additional sources.

additional.name: FishBase — Maylandia callainos
additional.notes: Cross-checked accepted scientific name (Maylandia callainos, Stauffer 2004), adult size (to 13 cm TL), temperature range, native range (Lake Malawi), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), and depth range. FishBase lists Metriaclima callainos as a synonym.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Mbuna Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Used for tank size recommendations, diet guidance (spirulina emphasis, Malawi bloat risk from high-protein diet), aggression management strategies (overstocking, heavy rockwork, female-heavy ratios), and water chemistry parameters for Lake Malawi species.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Lake Malawi cichlids
additional.notes: Background on mbuna ecology, aufwuchs diet, Lake Malawi water chemistry, mouthbrooding biology, and the Maylandia/Metriaclima nomenclature dispute. Referenced Konings and Stauffer (2018) continued use of Metriaclima.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Maylandia callainos species profile
additional.notes: Hobbyist community resource confirming isomorphic blue coloration in both sexes, hybridization concerns with M. estherae, typical clutch sizes (15–40 eggs), and holding duration (~21–28 days). Consistent with SeriouslyFish and Practical Fishkeeping data.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; female holds 15–40 eggs buccally for ~21–28 days. Spawning straightforward in a suitable mbuna setup; separating the holding female and avoiding hybridization with co-housed Maylandia species are the main challenges. Fry accept crushed spirulina flake immediately upon release.
